q: There is no compulsory military service in Canada.
opts: True
False
expl: The guide states there is no compulsory military service in Canada; serving in the forces is a noble way to contribute and an excellent career choice, but it is voluntary.

q: In what year was the Magna Carta signed in England?
expl: The guide says Canada's 800-year-old tradition of ordered liberty dates back to the signing of the Magna Carta in 1215 in England.

q: The Magna Carta is also known as:
opts: The Constitution Act
The British North America Act
The Great Charter of Freedoms
The Charter of Rights and Freedoms
expl: The guide notes the Magna Carta is also known as the Great Charter of Freedoms.

q: In what year was the Constitution of Canada amended to entrench the Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms?
expl: The Constitution of Canada was amended in 1982 to entrench the Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms.

q: In Canada, men and women are equal under the law.
opts: True
False
expl: The guide states plainly that in Canada, men and women are equal under the law.

q: Which of the following is a responsibility of Canadian citizenship?
opts: Serving on a jury when called to do so
Owning property in Canada
Belonging to a political party
Serving in the navy, army or air force
expl: Serving on a jury is listed among the citizenship responsibilities; when called to do so, you are legally required to serve. Military service is not compulsory in Canada.

q: What is habeas corpus?
opts: The right to vote in federal elections
The right to challenge unlawful detention by the state
The right to a free education
The right to live and work anywhere in Canada
expl: Habeas corpus is the right to challenge unlawful detention by the state, and it comes from English common law.

q: In the Charter, multiculturalism is described as:
opts: A temporary government program
A policy that applies only in large cities
A fundamental characteristic of the Canadian heritage and identity
A requirement that applies only to newcomers
expl: The guide describes multiculturalism as a fundamental characteristic of the Canadian heritage and identity; Canadians work hard to respect pluralism and live in harmony.

q: Under official language rights, which two languages have equal status in Parliament and throughout the government?
opts: English and Inuktitut
French and English
English and Spanish
French and Cree
expl: The guide states that French and English have equal status in Parliament and throughout the government.

q: Under mobility rights, Canadians can:
opts: Vote in any country's elections
Avoid paying provincial taxes
Claim land anywhere in Canada
Live and work anywhere they choose in Canada
expl: Mobility rights mean Canadians can live and work anywhere they choose in Canada, enter and leave the country freely, and apply for a passport.

q: What will you do at the citizenship ceremony?
opts: Take the Oath of Citizenship and receive your citizenship certificate
Write the citizenship test and receive a passport
Receive a Notice to Appear and pay a fee
Register to vote and receive a social insurance number
expl: At the ceremony you take the Oath of Citizenship, sign the oath form, and receive your Canadian Citizenship Certificate.

q: In the Oath of Citizenship, to whom do new citizens pledge to be faithful and bear true allegiance?
opts: The flag of Canada
The Constitution of Canada
The Queen of Canada, her heirs and successors
The Prime Minister of Canada
expl: The oath pledges faithfulness and true allegiance to Her Majesty the Queen of Canada, her heirs and successors; loyalty is professed to a person who represents all Canadians, not to a document, flag or country.

q: When called to serve on a jury, you are legally required to serve.
opts: True
False
expl: The guide states that when called to do so, you are legally required to serve on a jury.

q: Canada's openness and generosity extend to cultural practices such as forced marriage and other gender-based violence.
opts: True
False
expl: False. The guide says Canada's openness and generosity do not extend to barbaric cultural practices such as spousal abuse, 'honour killings', female genital mutilation, forced marriage or other gender-based violence.

q: Which of the following is one of the fundamental freedoms Canadians enjoy?
opts: Freedom to own firearms
Freedom from taxation
Freedom of peaceful assembly
Freedom of unlimited travel abroad
expl: Freedom of peaceful assembly is one of the fundamental freedoms, along with freedom of conscience and religion, freedom of thought, belief, opinion and expression, and freedom of association.

q: Voting in elections is described in the guide as:
opts: Optional for citizens over 55
A responsibility that comes with the right to vote
Required only in federal elections
A privilege reserved for those born in Canada
expl: The guide says the right to vote comes with a responsibility to vote in federal, provincial or territorial and local elections.

q: Which of the following is NOT listed as a source of Canadian law?
opts: English common law
The civil code of France
Laws passed by Parliament and the provincial legislatures
The United States Bill of Rights
expl: The sources of Canadian law are laws passed by Parliament and the provincial legislatures, English common law, the civil code of France, and the unwritten constitution inherited from Great Britain.

q: From where does habeas corpus come?
opts: English common law
The civil code of France
The 1982 Charter of Rights and Freedoms
The Citizenship Regulations
expl: The guide states that habeas corpus, the right to challenge unlawful detention by the state, comes from English common law.

q: Which list contains only fundamental freedoms identified in the guide?
opts: Freedom of conscience and religion; freedom of thought, belief, opinion and expression; freedom of peaceful assembly; freedom of association
Freedom of movement; freedom of trade; freedom of property; freedom of contract
Freedom of education; freedom of health care; freedom of housing; freedom of employment
Freedom of information; freedom of the seas; freedom of trade; freedom of worship
expl: The four fundamental freedoms are freedom of conscience and religion; freedom of thought, belief, opinion and expression (including speech and press); freedom of peaceful assembly; and freedom of association.

q: Freedom of speech and of the press is included within which fundamental freedom?
opts: Freedom of association
Freedom of peaceful assembly
Freedom of thought, belief, opinion and expression
Freedom of conscience and religion
expl: The guide lists freedom of thought, belief, opinion and expression as including freedom of speech and of the press.

q: What does the Charter say about Aboriginal Peoples' Rights?
opts: They expired when the Charter was entrenched in 1982
The Charter will not adversely affect any treaty or other rights of Aboriginal peoples
They apply only to First Nations, not to Inuit or Metis
They must be renegotiated every ten years
expl: The guide states that the rights guaranteed in the Charter will not adversely affect any treaty or other rights or freedoms of Aboriginal peoples.

q: The guide says Canadians enjoy an '800-year old tradition of ordered liberty' that dates back to:
opts: Confederation in 1867
The signing of the Magna Carta in 1215
The Quebec Act of 1774
The Constitution Act of 1982
expl: The sources of Canadian law together secure an 800-year-old tradition of ordered liberty dating back to the signing of the Magna Carta in 1215 in England.

q: Besides the regular Canadian Forces, which of the following does the guide mention as a way to help protect your community?
opts: Serving in the Coast Guard, a police force or a fire department
Paying extra income tax
Joining a federal political party
Working for a multinational corporation
expl: The guide says you may also serve in the Coast Guard or in emergency services in your community, such as a police force or fire department.

q: According to the guide, what can young people gain by getting involved in the cadets?
opts: A guaranteed career in the regular forces
Exemption from the citizenship test
Free university tuition
Discipline, responsibility and skills
expl: The guide says young people can learn discipline, responsibility and skills by getting involved in the cadets.

q: In the Oath of Citizenship, what do new citizens promise to faithfully observe?
opts: The traditions of the founding peoples
The decisions of the Prime Minister
The laws of Canada
The rulings of the Supreme Court
expl: In the oath, new citizens promise to faithfully observe the laws of Canada and fulfil their duties as a Canadian citizen.

q: According to the guide, in Canada we profess our loyalty to:
opts: A document, such as a constitution
A banner, such as a flag
A geopolitical entity, such as a country
A person who represents all Canadians, the Sovereign
expl: The guide explains that in Canada we profess loyalty to a person who represents all Canadians — the Sovereign (Queen or King) — and not to a document, a flag or a country.

q: The responsibility of protecting and enjoying our heritage and environment includes:
opts: Avoiding waste and pollution and protecting Canada's natural heritage
Donating to environmental charities each year
Purchasing only Canadian-made goods
Joining a provincial conservation service
expl: The guide says every citizen has a role to play in avoiding waste and pollution while protecting Canada's natural, cultural and architectural heritage for future generations.

q: Which of the following is given in the guide as an example of helping others in the community?
opts: Paying taxes on time
Volunteering at a food bank or other charity
Serving in the air reserves
Running for elected office
expl: Examples of helping others include helping people in need, assisting at your child's school, volunteering at a food bank or other charity, and encouraging newcomers to integrate.

q: What does the rule of law mean, according to the guide?
opts: Judges make all of Canada's laws
Only the federal government can pass laws
Individuals and governments are regulated by laws, not arbitrary actions
Police forces have final authority over the courts
expl: One of Canada's founding principles is the rule of law: individuals and governments are regulated by laws and not by arbitrary actions, and no person or group is above the law.

q: Which of the following is an example of taking responsibility for oneself and one's family?
opts: Voting in every election
Serving on a jury
Volunteering regularly at a local charity
Getting a job and taking care of one's family
expl: The guide says getting a job, taking care of one's family and working hard in keeping with one's abilities are important Canadian values, and work contributes to personal dignity, self-respect and Canada's prosperity.

q: The right to vote comes with a responsibility to vote in federal, provincial or territorial and local elections.
opts: True
False
expl: The guide lists voting in elections as a citizenship responsibility at the federal, provincial or territorial and local levels.

q: According to the guide, how does Canada respond to 'barbaric cultural practices' such as spousal abuse, 'honour killings', female genital mutilation or forced marriage?
opts: They are tolerated as part of Canada's openness and generosity
They are handled only by community leaders
Offenders are required to take citizenship classes
Those guilty of these crimes are severely punished under Canada's criminal laws
expl: The guide states that those guilty of these crimes are severely punished under Canada's criminal laws.

q: With which words does the Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms begin?
opts: 'We the people of Canada, in order to form a more perfect union...'
'Whereas Canada is founded upon principles that recognize the supremacy of God and the rule of law'
'Peace, order and good government...'
'Canada is a free and democratic society...'
expl: The Charter begins with the words 'Whereas Canada is founded upon principles that recognize the supremacy of God and the rule of law.'

q: According to the guide, the Charter's opening phrase underlines the importance of:
opts: Parliamentary supremacy and federalism
The separation of church and state
Religious traditions and the dignity and worth of the human person
Economic prosperity and free trade
expl: The guide says this phrase underlines the importance of religious traditions to Canadian society and the dignity and worth of the human person.

q: According to the guide, Canadians' rights and responsibilities come to us from our history, are secured by Canadian law, and reflect:
opts: Our shared traditions, identity and values
International human rights treaties
The will of the federal government
The customs of the British Empire alone
expl: The guide states that rights and responsibilities come from our history, are secured by Canadian law, and reflect our shared traditions, identity and values.

q: Which of the following is included in mobility rights?
opts: The right to free public transit
The right to dual citizenship
The right to a government job in any province
The right to enter and leave Canada freely and to apply for a passport
expl: Mobility rights include living and working anywhere in Canada, entering and leaving the country freely, and applying for a passport.

q: From which country did Canada inherit its unwritten constitution, one of the sources of Canadian law?
opts: France
The United States
Great Britain
The Netherlands
expl: The guide lists the unwritten constitution that Canada inherited from Great Britain among the sources of Canadian law.

q: Why is serving on a jury described as a privilege that makes the justice system work?
opts: Because jurors are paid generously for their service
Because the justice system depends on impartial juries made up of citizens
Because juries can overturn laws passed by Parliament
Because jury service replaces the need for judges
expl: The guide says serving on a jury is a privilege that makes the justice system work, as it depends on impartial juries made up of citizens.

q: In the Oath of Citizenship, the Constitution is described as recognizing and affirming the Aboriginal and treaty rights of:
opts: First Nations, Inuit and Metis peoples
Status Indians only
First Nations and Acadians
All Indigenous peoples of North America
expl: The oath includes observing the laws of Canada, including the Constitution, which recognizes and affirms the Aboriginal and treaty rights of First Nations, Inuit and Metis peoples.

q: Habeas corpus, the right to challenge unlawful detention, was created by the 1982 Charter of Rights and Freedoms.
opts: True
False
expl: False. Habeas corpus comes from English common law, not from the 1982 Charter.
